items.title: Chinese Steel Market Enters Long Plateau After Property Crash
items.summary: The Chinese steel market is navigating a prolonged weakening in demand rather than a cliff-drop collapse, as the steep decline from property is increasingly offset by manufacturing and exports, according to experts at an industry conference last week.

items.title: Iranian Crude Oil Exports Surge Via Hormuz as More Ships Transit
items.summary: Iran has ramped up the amount of crude oil it openly sends through the Strait of Hormuz to the highest since the war started, as shipping activity in the region picks up while Tehran and Washington work toward a lasting peace deal.

items.title: With a Fragile Ceasefire under Threat, What Future for the Strait of Hormuz?
items.summary: The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran prompted Tehran to close the Strait of Hormuz, allowing only selected ships to pass through. In this Q&A, Crisis Group experts Yasmine Farouk, Chris Newton and Ali Vaez consider what could come next in this vital maritime trade corridor.

items.title: Thai Shipper Remains Wary of Hormuz Despite Ceasefire
items.summary: Bangkok-based Precious Shipping Pcl, whose vessel was struck in March by Iranian projectiles in the Strait of Hormuz, said it remains cautious about sending ships through one of the world’s most important energy and trade chokepoints.

EU and NATO Members Grapple with War in Iran
items.summary: This week on War & Peace, Olga speaks with Crisis Group’s head of EU Affairs, Lisa Musiol, about how the EU, its members and European members of NATO have responded to the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran and what it reveals about EU foreign policy and its constraints.

items.title: Escrivá Says ECB Must Be Vigilant on Oil Price Impact on Wages
items.summary: The European Central Bank must watch for any knock-on impact on wages from higher oil and commodity prices resulting from the Middle East conflict, Governing Council member Jose Luis Escrivá said.

items.title: South Korea’s ex-justice minister jailed for 25 years over martial law bid
items.summary:
A court sentenced a former South Korean justice minister on Monday to 25 years in prison for his role in ex-president Yoon Suk-yeol’s brief and disastrous declaration of martial law in 2024.
Park Sung-jae was found guilty of involvement in “insurrection”, the Yonhap news agency reported from the Seoul Central District Court.
Yoon’s December 2024 martial law declaration lasted only about six hours

Did Myanmar-China talks spawn a more emboldened junta?
items.summary:
A recent meeting between the leaders of Myanmar and China has given the military junta an opening to persuade Asean to allow it to return to the bloc’s summits.
Analysts also say, however, that Myanmar’s regime could feel emboldened to escalate action against resistance forces, revving up its “military approach” to deal with the country’s civil war.

items.title: China defends role in global supply chains amid Strait of Hormuz crisis
items.summary:
China’s proactive role in stabilising global energy and fertiliser markets amid the Strait of Hormuz crisis shows the country is a defender of global supply chains, rather than a disruptive force as “hyped” by the West, Vice-Premier Ding Xuexiang said on Monday.
